## reference · perceived performance optimistic UI skeleton motion microinteractions response time feedback
* 0.1 second is the limit for having the user feel that the system is reacting instantaneously.
* 1.0 second is the limit for the user's flow of thought to stay uninterrupted, even though the user will notice the delay.
* 10 seconds is the limit for keeping the user's attention focused on the dialogue, after which users will want to perform other tasks while waiting.
* For delays of more than 0.1 but less than 1.0 second, no special feedback is necessary, but the user does lose the feeling of operating directly on the data.
* For delays of more than 1 second, indicate to the user that the computer is working on the problem.
* For delays of more than 10 seconds, provide a percent-done indicator and a clearly signposted way for the user to interrupt the operation.
* Use a "busy" cursor and a rapidly changing number to indicate progress for delays between 2 and 10 seconds.
* Provide continuous feedback to the user in the form of a percent-done indicator for operations taking more than about 10 seconds.
* Use a graphic progress bar instead of just stating the expected remaining time in numbers.
* For operations where it is unknown in advance how much work has to be done, provide running progress feedback in terms of the absolute amount of work done.
* Use a less specific progress indicator, such as a spinning ball or busy bee, if a percent-done indicator is not possible.
Sources: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/response-times-3-important-limits/
